sentences.text: Well, as I was saying that the Germans made him a slave laborer to work at the Brenner Pass.
And it was a horrible experience for him and his family.
They barely survived.
And they were able to make it to Germany.
And then he contacted me, and he was able to come to the United States.
But this is how I learned about the slave labor that was used widely in Germany.
In fact, there was one German concentration camp commander who was renting slave labor to the nearby city to his concentration camp, and putting this money in his account.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And his name was Koch, and his wife was the notorious Ilse Koch.
And I was surprised to find out that the lawyer from the SS went after him.
And the SS refused to even listen to this lawyer.
And he finally made his way to Himmler.
And finally, Himmler did arrest this fellow, Koch, and he was executed.
But Ilse Koch who was not.
She survived.
Well, I had several jobs.
First, I was doing translation.
Then I was reviewer.
And finally, I got a job as a research analyst.
And research analyst was a job to work with the American lawyers on the prosecution staff.
Many American lawyers did not know any German.
All the documents that we captured were written in German.
Particularly, some of them were written in the Gothic script, which is not taught in our American schools.
So even the Americans who knew German, they couldn't work on these documents.
So I had to work a lot of overtime.
And-- But we did-- we captured many documents.
In fact, the whole file of the Gestapo was neatly in alphabetical order, for Himmler did not try to burn it.
And we had to read the document.
And attached to it, a so-called analysis sheet, to describe, in English, what the document is all about, particularly the highlights.
And then we had to sign the analysis sheet, attach to the document, and give it to the lawyer.

And the lawyer would read our sheet of paper and then decide which part of the document should be introduced as evidence in court, and then send it to the translating division to have a certified translation.
Sometimes we had to do some interpreting, like I did interrupting on the Krupp trial, which was stopped, and Krupp went back to business.
So that was one trial that was stopped in the middle.
But I was working on the main trial against the Nazi government.
And I saw all those members of the Nazi government.
And one man that did impress me was Goering.
Goering was quite deflated, but he is the only one who had the guts to recognize his crimes.
He talked for two days in his defense, and he said that, if I had to start it all over again, I would do the same thing because I did it for the greatness of my people.
All of the other were saying, well, we followed the orders of our Fuhrer.
We didn't have our will, so we are innocent.
Well, in fact, Ribbentrop went even so far when he was asked, did you drive a certain road in Berlin to work?
He says, yes, I did.
Did you [INAUDIBLE] that concentration camp that was outskirts of Berlin?
He says, no, that's not a concentration camp.
That was a home for retired Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ribbentrop was saying, anyway.
But it's interesting how three people were acquitted.
One man was Fritzsche, from the Propagandaministerium.
He was caught by the Red Army, but the Red Army surrendered him to the Nuremberg trial.
And he was acquitted, and the Soviet delegation was awfully mad that we acquitted him.
Then the-- the financial wizard, Helmer Schacht, was acquitted.
And the other one was von Pappen, a diplomat who was an ambassador in Turkey.
He was defended by his own son, who was a lawyer.
Several lawyers were defending him.
And he was free.
So three were acquitted, and others got 20 years and so on.
One fellow who was-- I think he was honest was that fellow who-- the architect.
He wrote, even, a book.
Speer, Albert Speer.
He was a young man without a job.
And the Nazi party offered him a job, so he joined it.
He was horrified what was done to the Jews.
And he definitely was sorry what was done to innocent people.
So he came up-- he was not hiding.
I mean, he came out into the open.
That's why he got only 20 years.
But others, like, for example, Streicher, who was in charge of an antisemitic paper, he [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] definitely denied many of his crimes.
His farm that was on the outskirts of Nuremberg was taken over by victims, Jewish victims.
And they had the Israeli flag there.
And they were learning farming on his farm.
But Streicher, when he was hanged, his last word was Purim.
I don't know why he said Purim.
Somebody told me that Purim is what, execution or something?
Has to do something with execution or something.
I don't know what Purim means.
But that was his last words that he uttered when he was hanged.
So I know that Goebbels did manage to commit suicide because he was a famous pilot in World War I.
He was with the Richthofen squadron.
And he was-- when Dr.
Richthofen was shot down, the Red Baron, he took over.
And so he was also in charge of the German aviation during World War II.
And he didn't want to be hanged.
He says, why don't you shoot me?
And we said, nothing doing.
Criminals will be hanged.
So he-- I was told that he hid his poison pill in the toothpaste, because every prisoner that we took, we searched them thoroughly.
Because each Nazi member had a pill hidden in his hair, in his rectum, in his mouth.
And so if you-- in fact, the story about Himmler is that he tried to assume a false identity as a soldier, and he came to the British checkpoint to go to Denmark.
And the soldier suddenly noticed on the passport that, two days ago, Himmler signed his passport.
And he says, Herr Snyder, you wait here.
I have to go to my officer to check your passport.
And so he went to the officer and he says, he saw Himmler two days ago, and we are looking for him.
And the officer says, well, I don't speak any German, so we have to call the British intelligence officer.
So as soon as the British intelligence officer appeared, he said, did you search him?
He says, no, we didn't.
Oh, you better search him.
Let's search him.
So as soon as they approached him to search, they heard a crushing sound in his mouth.
He crushed a pill, then dropped dead.
So we missed Himmler at the Nuremberg trial.
He managed to commit suicide.
Another fellow that is still a mystery is Bormann.
Bormann took the place of Hess.
And he was a deputy Fuhrer, Bormann.
Now, there are many theories about Bormann.
One German woman, when Berlin was occupied by the Red Army, she saw Bormann in the Soviet headquarters.
Our version is that Bormann tried to escape, and that he was killed on the streets of Berlin.
And the Germans claim that they have found his bones.
However, the Soviet intelligence says that no, Bormann escaped to Argentina and was in charge of the Nazi party.
Now, the testimony of that woman, some people thought that Bormann was an agent of Stalin, who gave Soviet Union some valuable information that was known only by Bormann and Hitler.
I don't think that Hitler would send the information to Stalin.
So Bormann is a very controversial-- in fact, some people maintain that Bormann took a leave from Argentina to visit his wife and children in Germany, naturally under an assumed name.
Another mystery at the Nuremberg would be Hess.
Hess is quite a mystery, because the British doctor that examined him in Spandau, according to the records of World War I, he was bayoneted into-- in the chest.
And the doctor was looking for that wound.
Nothing was there.
Then another thing is that if you-- a film was taken when Hess took off in the Messerschmitt to fly to England.
And when you look at that photograph, you see the serial number of that airplane.
And he was accomplished pilot.
However, the air plane that crashed in England, the pilot was incompetent, crashed [INAUDIBLE].
And that serial number is different.
So there was a switch.
And theory is that probably Hess was arrested by Himmler and killed, and a double was sent in there.
Because many people, Nazi members that were there at the Nuremberg trial, were asking questions of him-- of Hess.
And Hess would say, I don't remember, I don't remember, and so on.
Then what happened to Hess, he got a life sentence to spend in Spandau.
He was all alone while the others served their sentences.
And each week, somebody else was guarding the Spandau prison.
Once they were the French, then the British, then the Americans, then the Soviets.
And when the Americans were guarding Hess, a Britisher came in and said to the man that was watching Hess, he says, you have a telephone call.
And so the American left Hess there with the Englishman and went to answer the telephone.
And when he returned back, he found Hess dead with a little wire around his neck.
Now, you try to commit suicide that way.
Put a wire around your neck and try to commit suicide.
What would happen?
You would pass out before you die.
So somebody had to help him.
But official now, they say he committed suicide.
It's a very curious suicide.
[INAUDIBLE] So that's another mystery that we have from the Nuremberg trial, one that has not been yet researched.
Or maybe it's top secret.
I don't know.
But it's very curious about these members of the Nuremberg trial.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
So there-- also, we did try to show the Germans that our trial is fair, because we used the-- what America believes in-- that you are not guilty until you are proven to be guilty.
And the Soviet delegation was very mad at us.
They says, we know that they're guilty.
Let's hang them and have it over with.
We said, nothing doing.
We can't do that.
And so what happened is that the German defense counsels were given assistance to try to prove the innocence of their clients.
They would be given trips, and witnesses would be brought in.
So we gave them a whole chance to prove the innocence of the victims.
To top that, we also showed the Nazi justice, because you probably know, on July 20, there was a conspiracy to kill Hitler.
And many people were involved in this conspiracy.
Even the commander of Berlin, von Witzleben, was involved.
Rommel was involved too.
And so, the Germans had a so-called Volksgericht, a people's trial.
And they filmed the proceedings.
And we showed this German film to the Nazis, how their justice worked.
And what you see is von Witzleben was carried into the court.
He can't even walk, the poor fellow.
And he grabs on the seat there, and he faces the judge.
And the judge is calling him Schweinehund and so on, and traitor, and doesn't give Witzleben a chance to say anything in his defense.
Forget a defense lawyer.
There was no defense lawyer.
OK, sentence to death.
And it was carried out.
That was the justice of the Nazis.
And he was sentenced.
Rommel was very popular in Germany.
He is a so-called Desert Fox, you know, in Africa and so on.
He performed miracles in Africa.
And so-- but he was involved in this conspiracy.
So Gestapo agents came to his home when he was wounded around the channel there that he was in command.
And he was told, OK, you have a choice.
Either you go on trial, even if it is not very popular, the trial, but we-- we will try you as a traitor and your family will go to prison.
Or you take these pills and commit suicide.
And get your papers in order.
And he talked to his son, who is now the mayor of Stuttgart, and got his papers in order, took those pills, and died.
The official information was that he died of his wounds.
And he was given a hero's burial, and you see his son is now the mayor.
So they had-- their-- their justice was completely different than ours.
So we tried to impress them that we were trying, bending, to be fair.
Naturally, many people escaped.
We-- we didn't catch everybody.
Even if we occupied all of Germany, they escaped because some agencies of our government were working against the Nuremberg Trial.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Like, for example, that fellow from Lyon, France, the SS man who was being used by our counter-intelligence to start getting information about the Soviet Union.
But the German courts are very lenient to these criminals.
And they are not as harsh as the Nuremberg trial was.
Even the war crime trials in Tokyo was quite lenient.
Like, for example, a Japanese general who was using PWs as guinea pigs.
He was given a high position and salary in the Pentagon to teach us those methods.
So Nuremberg was criticized that we introduced a law that did not exist before.
And Michael Kremen, senior [INAUDIBLE], he says, how could you make it retroactive, crime against humanity?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
But the other indictments that we had, they were quite valid.
They were definitely valid, because after World War I, many of those war criminals were put into plush German jails, and lived in luxury, and then, for good behavior, they were released.
[LAUGHS]
So at Nuremberg, we were trying to punish the war criminals, so that it would be a warning against other crimes that are occurring, even today.
Like, for example, to attack a country without a declaration of war.
That's why the Nuremberg trial is not very popular, right, because everybody is starting a war without a declaration of war, right?
You just march in and make war.
Or, the Germans did destroy the village of Lidice in Czechoslovakia as a-- and the same method is being used today too, right?
The same method that we condemned in Nuremberg is being used today.
So that's why I don't know what that trial in the Hague, Holland is going to face, whether they can do the-- something to stop the war crimes, because there are war crimes still occurring even today.
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yeah.
What was your impression of some of the American attorneys that were there?
Do you have an opinion about the judges, the attorneys, your colleagues?
Can you tell me about this?
Well, unfortunately, some of the lawyers didn't know the German history or the German background, which handicapped them.
Just like today we don't know too much about Bosnia, right?
And we make a lot of errors.
You have to know-- even American businessman are not very successful abroad because they do not know the history of the people and the-- what the people like.
I met a German salesman in Bulgaria.
Boy, he spoke fluent Bulgarian.
He knew the Bulgarian history.
And he always tried to please the client.
And people are quite impressed if you speak their language.
They definitely are.
We had one fellow who was in charge of the gas ovens in the SS.
And he was sitting in the visitors' gallery, watching the trial against the SS that I was working on.
And one of the Germans who was on trial tried to tell the MP that the fellow in charge of the gas ovens is sitting in the visitors' gallery.
But the MP didn't speak any German, so we had to call an interpreter.
By the time we called the interpreter, the fellow was not there anymore.
He saw the commotion, and the British told us, be patient.
Let's follow him.
Because he had a room rented right next to the court house.
And he came to-- [LAUGHS] to watch the trial of his friends, coworkers.
And-- but you know, the Americans are impatient, and we had to arrest him.
We busted into that room, and we found a map of all the concentration camps, a picture of Hitler there.
And naturally, we put him on trial.
But the British were awfully mad.
He says, there are others who are in cahoots with him.
You know, we could capture the others.
He had, naturally, false identification papers.
He has never been a Nazi.
He was de-Nazified.
[LAUGHS] So it really-- we did make a few mistakes there too, you know?

And the-- the Germans, when they were-- like the Oberkommando of the Wehrmacht, the army, when they were very informed about the atrocities committed by the Gestapo and Volkpolizei in eastern territories, the General [?
Scherwent ?] received a report from Major Foss who, with his regiment, got quarters in Voronezh.
I still see that document in-- And that major says, I am a veteran of World War I.
I fought in the invasion of Poland and France.
But I have never seen such atrocities as I saw in the Soviet Union.
He says that, as they got to their quarters, there were some shots fired at the outskirts.
So every soldiers with their camera ran there have to take pictures to see what's going on.
So Foss and his staff went, also, to see what's going on.
And what he saw, it was a common grave, a big hole in the ground.
And people were marched to the edge of that hole, shot, fell into the hole.
And then another group taken in-- civilians.
And particularly, he was impressed by an old man, very distinguished, with a white beard, who was still alive in that ditch.
So Major Foss approached the commander of the execution squad and says, don't you have the decency to finish him off?
He says, oh, that Russian swine already [INAUDIBLE].
He will be buried anyway, so we are not going to waste any bullets on him.
Well, this is the report that he sends to the Oberkommando of the Wehrmacht, Foss.
He says, I've never seen such atrocity.
And General [?
Scherwent, ?] on the edge of that report, writes, this execution should take place at night.
Because too many Germans are taking snapshots and sending home, and people at home will find out about these atrocities.
That's the comment the general who was in the Oberkommando in the Wehrmacht.
This is the reaction of the Wehrmacht to the atrocities committed.
You've probably have heard about the [INAUDIBLE], right?
It's the same thing.
But here, there was-- some decent Germans tried to protest.
And look how-- what kind of an answer he got.
It's-- Because they had to make-- they had to make room in PW camp, they would take people to execution.
In particular, they would choose-- there was a SS report that I read, where in a PW camp, another group was arriving.
So they had to make room.
So they chose PWs that lost a hand or a foot-- you know, invalids.
And they say, well, for invalids, we don't need a strong guard.
We can fewer men to supervise this execution.
So they drove outside the city.
They forced some of these prisoners to dig that big hole in the ground.
And there was one man per truck with a submachine gun to guard the prisoners.
Those were prisoners of war.
And what happened, it was winter, and the SS had a very heavy clothing.
And so one of the PWs jumped the German SS man and knocked him down, and took his submachine gun, and opened the fire against the other SS men.
And the other SS man had to jump into that hole to escape alive.
But many of them were shot, because these prisoners, they take the arm from the-- and they fled.
And so, this was a report written how several SS men were killed, he says, because of this-- and they describe the execution in detail, you know, the execution of PWs.
They themselves acknowledge that PWs were-- but the only excuse that they had is that Soviet Union has never signed the Geneva Convention.
So, therefore, legally, we couldn't do anything about that.
But it's interesting.
We had the document of Admiral Canaris, who was the chief of the German counter-intelligence.
I saw that.
But it didn't have anything to do with my case, so I just looked through it.
And this Admiral Canaris, in his personal diary, was talking about some double agents that were working for the Allies and for the Nazis.
And a professor here in Washington told me, I'm looking for the diary of Admiral Canaris.
I said, we had it at Nuremberg.
Says, it disappeared.
So many documents that we had in Nuremberg are disappearing.
OK.
OK.
[LAUGHS]
